summary: Gruber examines why Apple's built-in system apps can't be deleted from iOS and why their updates are tied to OS releases. He argues that iOS 4's folders feature is the best solution for hiding unwanted apps, since deletion would create UX inconsistencies. He extends the analysis to Mac OS X, noting that Apple bundles app updates with major OS releases partly for marketing reasons — to pad the feature count of new OS versions. He suggests that as OS X matures, Apple may need to decouple app updates from system updates, as they already do with Safari.
key_points: Deleting built-in iOS apps creates a recovery problem since there's no App Store mechanism to re-download them, and having two different removal methods would be un-iPhone-like
iBooks shipping as an App Store app rather than a system app was strategic — it allows Apple to update it independently of iOS releases
Apple bundles system app updates with major OS releases partly as a marketing strategy to inflate the feature count of new OS versions
Safari is the sole exception to Apple's pattern, shipping major updates independently of Mac OS X releases
As Mac OS X matures and major system updates become less frequent, decoupling app updates from OS updates may become necessary
money_quotes: It's simpler and more obvious for there to be one and only one way to do it, and simply leave the system apps as unremovable.
Users aren't the only ones who are constricted by iOS's system apps being tied to the system as a whole. Apple is too.
A bug fix in one system app — Mail, Safari, Weather, whatever — doesn't ship to users until the next release of the entire OS.
Major updates to Mail and iChat, issued now for Mac OS X 10.6.x, would take a lot of punch out of 10.7 next year.
As Mac OS X matures, though, and major updates to the system as a whole become less frequent, it might make sense for Apple to start decoupling these application updates from system updates.
I think the new 'folders' feature is the best you home screen neatniks are going to get.
key_insight: Apple's bundling of app updates with OS releases constrains both users and Apple itself, serving marketing goals at the expense of agility, and this tension will force decoupling as the platforms mature.
